title: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case)
summary: Following the oleum gas leak from Shriram Foods and Fertiliser Industries in Delhi, M.C. Mehta filed a PIL. The Supreme Court laid down the doctrine of Absolute Liability: an enterprise engaged in hazardous activity is absolutely liable to pay compensation if any harm results — with no exceptions as under the English rule of strict liability from Rylands v. Fletcher.
key_holdings: Absolute liability doctrine: no exceptions apply to enterprises causing harm through hazardous activity
Enterprise must be absolutely liable, regardless of whether harm occurred despite all precautions
Amount of compensation should be commensurate with the magnitude and capacity of enterprise
Public interest litigation is a powerful tool to enforce environmental rights
key_quotes: We are of the view that an enterprise which is engaged in a hazardous or inherently dangerous industry... owes an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community.
We do not think that the rule of strict liability as evolved in Rylands v Fletcher is adequate for modern conditions.
why_important: Created India's unique doctrine of absolute liability for hazardous industries — stronger than English strict liability. Foundation of modern Indian environmental law.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Environmental liability principles affect corporate financial reporting, insurance, and compliance costs.
